input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10 th‑scale, teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor suite and control electronics. The physical twin’s architecture is divided into lower and upper chassis layers, alongside dedicated autonomy modules that process sensor data to control vehicle motion. The vehicle operates in a controlled test track environment, enabling high‑fidelity measurement of adaptive cruise control (ACC) performance. Its key characteristics for digital twinning include modular hardware layers enabling easy access for telemetry acquisition, real‑time sensor streams that can be mirrored in the twin, and a dedicated communication interface for offloading compute from the resource‑constrained on‑board ECU to external cloud equipment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin system delivers three core services: 1) **Data Monitoring** – a user interface (UI) that displays real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and provides mechanisms to view, save, and generate reports of historic data. 2) **Computation Offloading** – a cloud‑based ACC module that receives raw sensor telemetry, determines the lead vehicle, computes desired speed based on distance metrics, and sends the resulting control command back to the vehicle for actuation, thereby relieving the on‑board ECU of heavy processing. 3) **Remote Management** – the same UI allows operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed manually, and issue emergency braking commands, all over a low‑end‑to‑end‑latency communication link that maintains adherence to safety‑critical constraints.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin implementation relies on a low‑latency, bi‑directional communication stack between the vehicle and a cloud compute platform, a data collection and persistence layer for logging telemetry, and a reporting engine for generating analytics. The UI leverages a web or embedded front‑end that visualizes real‑time state vectors, projected trajectories, and object tracking overlays. Compute tasks (leader detection, speed calculation) are executed in the cloud to leverage higher processing power, while critical safety responses are routed back to the vehicle in real time. The system thus integrates sensor data acquisition, cloud offloading, UI visualization, and actuated command transmission to realize a fully autonomous digital twin with human‑in‑the‑loop capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin System requires all component clocks to be synchronized with an accuracy of 100 ms. Network latency from the vehicle to the Digital Twin Entity must not exceed 100 ms. This 100 ms bound is specifically chosen due to hardware limitations, although stricter synchronization (<100 ms) is normally mandated for safety‑critical vehicular applications.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin architecture is arranged into three hierarchical entities—User Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and Physical Twin Entity—each instantiated on separate hardware nodes, creating a decentralized deployment where the Physical Twin hosts compute, sensors and actuators, the Digital Twin manages data and control logic, and the User Entity provides visualization and control panels. A single digital twin instance per vehicle encapsulates these responsibilities, with explicit interfaces coordinating tasks across entities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows ISO 23247 Reference Architecture. Each Functional Element (FE) is implemented as an individual ROS 2 package, which may contain one or more ROS 2 nodes. This mapping aligns the software components with the ISO 23247 entity-based structure, resulting in three distinct entities: User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin. The User Entity hosts the User Interface FE split into a 3D Visualization FE and a Control Panel FE. The Digital Twin Entity houses data storage, preprocessing, and control logic. The Physical Twin Entity interfaces directly with the vehicle’s compute, sensors, and actuators and executes compute‑heavy functions such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The ISO 23247 Part 2 reference architecture defines an entity‑based Digital Twin system in which an Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) is represented by a Digital Twin Entity.  Computational models inside this entity encapsulate the OME’s physical state and behavior, receiving sensor inputs and transmitting control commands via the Data Collection and Device Control Entity.  The standard does not prescribe specific model types (geometric, behavioral, analytical) or detailed data‑management schemes, but it allows interoperability through protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture.  Functional Entities within the Digital Twin Entity micro‑services implement model relationships and responsibilities such as data interpretation, simulation, and user‑interaction interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from physical sensors including LiDAR raw point cloud, vehicle state, and motor/steering telemetry is transmitted to the Digital Twin via a ROS2 network. The Data Translation component serializes the data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. Raw LiDAR point cloud is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component, creating object tracks and projected driving paths that are displayed in real‑time on the 3D Visualization. Event triggers are sensor data updates; communication uses UDP over Wi‑Fi.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the digital twin (e.g., desired speed or emergency braking) are encapsulated as UDP packets via the Wi‑Fi network and delivered to the vehicle’s actuation controllers. Commands are processed by the Data Translation component which formats the message, performs unit‑conversions, and aligns message types. Vehicle actuation hardware executes the commands, and acknowledgment messages reporting execution status are sent back to the digital twin, ensuring round‑trip confirmation. Conflict resolution between concurrent commands is handled by the control‑panel FE, which prioritises safety‑critical instructions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Software components communicate over a ROS2 network, while inter‑entity communication occurs over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP as the transport layer. The Data Translation component (ISO 23247 Data Translation FE) manages serialization, message type alignment, and unit conversions across the network.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Model‑based decision support is embedded in the digital twin through an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) module that continuously computes the desired vehicle speed by detecting the lead vehicle, measuring inter‑vehicle distance, and applying control algorithms derived from the twin’s physical model. The twin captures real‑time sensor data from the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), transforms it into human‑interpretable metrics, and displays it via a user interface that allows operators to view live status, generate and save reports, and remotely adjust ACC parameters. Decision making is thus a closed‑loop process where the twin updates the control logic based on telemetry, while the user interface provides transparent feedback and override capability.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing, particularly Part 2 (reference architecture). The standard describes an entity‑based architecture comprising an Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), a Data Collection and Device Control Entity, a Digital Twin Entity, and a User Entity, each composed of Functional Entities (FEs) that perform tasks such as actuating the OME, transforming data, and interfacing with users. While ISO 23247 does not prescribe implementation details, it recommends interoperability protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for communication between functional entities.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

However, it mentions the vehicle operates in a 'controlled test track environment,' whereas the source text specifically refers to a 'real-world setting' or 'case study... in a real-world setting,' without explicitly naming a test track.

The description is a perfect match for the three services defined in the source text. It details Data Monitoring (viewing state, tracks, paths, and reporting), Computation Offloading (ACC logic moving to the cloud/DT and returning commands), and Remote Management (UI commands for enable/disable, speed, and braking) with high fidelity.

The extraction provides a strong technical summary of the implementation details, correctly identifying the communication stack (ROS2 and Wi-Fi), the persistence layer (SQL database), and the reporting engine. It accurately describes the flow of data and the role of the cloud for compute offloading. While it infers 'web or embedded' for the UI front-end (which isn't explicitly stated, only 'User Interface FE' is), the rest of the technical description is highly specific and well-supported.

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

It correctly states that the standard does not prescribe specific model types and allows interoperability via protocols like OPC UA. The description is well-supported by the text: 'The Digital Twin Entity digitally represents the OME,' 'The Data Collection and Device Control Entity is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the OME,' and 'The standard does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.' However, it could include more specific details from the case study, such as the use of 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' and 'Kalman filter' models. Overall, it is highly accurate and grounded in the sources.

The source documents explicitly describe physical acting components, such as 'Motor,' 'Steering Servo,' and 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller,' and mention an 'Actuation' component that converts desired speed into motor voltage commands. Therefore, stating 'Not in Document' is incorrect, as the relevant information is present.

The source documents mention sensing components, including 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR' and 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera,' and describe their use in the Physical Twin Entity. Thus, stating 'Not in Document' is incorrect, as the evidence is present.

The source documents do not provide detailed information about model fidelity requirements, validation methods, uncertainty quantification, or quality assurance processes. The only mention of fidelity is in the general definition of Digital Twins as 'real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections,' but no specific considerations are discussed. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is correct.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
